expected: >=1 tradition provides vocabulary for gaps; predict reliabilism identifies ~2 untracked failure modes
actual: All 4 traditions identify gaps. Reliabilism identifies 3 untracked failure modes (process reliability tracking, method-level calibration, environmental sensitivity). Social epistemology is the most gap-rich tradition with 5 unaddressed concepts. Total: 16 gaps, 9 actionable.
diff: Reliabilism exceeded prediction (3 vs ~2 gaps). Social epistemology was unexpectedly the largest gap source — no tool addresses testimony, peer disagreement, or epistemic injustice. Bayesian tradition was best-covered (only 2 gaps). Popperian tradition revealed the swarm has implicit falsificationism but no explicit demarcation criterion or degree-of-corroboration metric.

mapped_tools.maps_to: Refutation detection — signals UNCHALLENGED/CONFIRM-ONLY/STALE-TEST identify claims that resist refutation. prescribe() generates concrete falsification actions. AXIOM-BY-DESIGN vs empirical distinction mirrors demarcation problem.
mapped_tools.coverage: partial — detects absence of refutation but does not actively generate conjectures or track refutation attempts

mapped_tools.maps_to: Falsification conditions — every belief requires 'Falsified if' field (check_format). cascade_check traces downstream effects when a belief is falsified.
mapped_tools.coverage: good — structural enforcement of falsifiability criterion

mapped_tools.maps_to: Tracks FALSIFIED verdict class with BF=0.1, replication consistency checks identify contradictory evidence across experiments.
mapped_tools.coverage: partial — records falsification events but through Bayesian lens, not Popperian corroboration

gaps.description: Popper's central contribution: distinguishing scientific from non-scientific claims. No tool classifies claims as testable vs untestable. dogma_finder AXIOM-STUCK is closest but does not enforce a boundary — axioms coexist with empirical claims without formal separation.

Popper distinguished corroboration (survived severe tests) from confirmation (accumulated positive evidence). bayes_meta.py tracks confirmation via Bayes factors but has no severity-of-test metric. A claim that survived a weak test and one that survived a severe test get equal BF=10.

Popperian epistemology values bold, risky predictions over safe ones. No tool tracks the riskiness or boldness of frontier hypotheses. bayes_meta quality scoring gives 0.2 for falsification conditions but does not measure how much the hypothesis risks.

novel_swarm: FOUNDING-ERA signal (dogma_finder) — Popper does not distinguish claim age as epistemically relevant; the swarm treats founding-era claims as epistemically weaker by default
REFINE-DRIFT signal — detecting language softening without substantive change has no Popperian analog; Popper focused on logical content, not rhetorical drift

mapped_tools.maps_to: Direct implementation of Bayesian updating. Sequential prior-to-posterior computation with quality-weighted Bayes factors. Calibration analysis (predicted P vs actual rate). Publication bias detection via quality-confirmation correlation.
mapped_tools.coverage: strong — the swarm's most tradition-aligned tool

mapped_tools.maps_to: Evidence weighting (evidence_weight component) assigns prior-like weights to different evidence types. Recency decay is analogous to temporal discounting of evidence.
mapped_tools.coverage: partial — uses weights but not formal probability distributions

mapped_tools.maps_to: STALE_WINDOW=50 sessions as evidence decay threshold is an implicit prior on evidence relevance. Citation recency tracking is informal evidence aggregation.
mapped_tools.coverage: weak — classification is categorical, not probabilistic

gaps.description: bayes_meta.py offers --empirical flag for base rate prior but no systematic sensitivity analysis. With many single-experiment frontiers, posteriors are prior-dominated. The tool acknowledges this but does not quantify it.

Bayesian epistemology uses strictly proper scoring rules (Brier, log) to evaluate probabilistic predictions. bayes_meta.py computes ECE but not Brier scores or log-likelihood. ECE bins are coarse (5 bins) and the actual-truth proxy is crude (fraction of CONFIRMED verdicts).

novel_swarm: Concordance penalty — bayes_meta.py pulls posteriors toward 0.5 when evidence splits between CONFIRMED and FALSIFIED. Addresses order-dependence in sequential updating, which standard Bayesian theory ignores (ideal Bayesian updating is order-independent).
Replication cap (L-909) — capping posterior at 0.85 with <3 experiments is a structural humility mechanism absent from standard Bayesian epistemology.

mapped_tools.maps_to: Independence factor (0.5 for self-referential, 1.0 for external) is a crude process-reliability indicator. External evidence gets higher weight because external processes are more reliable than internal self-assessment.
mapped_tools.coverage: weak — binary rather than tracking actual process reliability

mapped_tools.maps_to: MUST-KNOW classification (items referenced by boot files and active tools) identifies operationally load-bearing beliefs — a form of tracking which beliefs are produced by reliable (tested) processes.
mapped_tools.coverage: indirect — tracks operational importance, not process reliability

mapped_tools.maps_to: Quality scoring (score_quality) partially tracks process reliability: external benchmarks, statistical methods, falsification conditions. Publication bias check assesses systematic distortion in the evidence-production process.
mapped_tools.coverage: partial — quality is a reliability proxy but coarse (5 binary indicators summed)

gaps.description: Reliabilism's core claim: justified belief = belief from a reliable process. No swarm tool tracks the reliability of specific cognitive/tool processes. Does orient.py reliably identify the right next task? Does compact.py reliably preserve important information? Each tool has implicit reliability but it is never measured.

Goldman's reliabilism distinguishes reliable from unreliable belief-forming methods. The swarm uses multiple methods (experiment, lesson extraction, principle distillation, challenge resolution) but never calibrates them against each other. Which method produces more accurate beliefs?

A reliable process in one environment may be unreliable in another. The swarm operates in different environments (high-concurrency vs single-session, early-stage vs mature) but no tool tracks whether processes reliable at N=200 lessons remain reliable at N=1200.

novel_swarm: Decay-based de-justification — grounding_audit.py and knowledge_state.py automatically degrade justification over time. Reliabilism typically treats reliability as a static property of a process; the swarm treats it as temporally bounded.

mapped_tools.maps_to: SELF-REFERENTIAL signal detects when evidence chains are entirely internal — a weak proxy for social epistemology's concern about epistemic bubbles and groupthink.
mapped_tools.coverage: very weak — keyword regex, and the swarm is fundamentally a single-agent system

mapped_tools.maps_to: Publication bias check is a social-epistemic concern: are we systematically selecting which experiments to run or report? Quality-confirmation correlation is a structural bias detector.
mapped_tools.coverage: partial — detects one specific social bias but not broader testimony/authority dynamics

gaps.description: Social epistemology's central question: when is it rational to believe based on testimony? The swarm has synthetic steerers and human input but no formal model of testimonial trust. Human directives in MEMORY.md are treated as authoritative without calibration. Steerer signals are weighted by tier but the tier system is self-assigned.

When two epistemic peers disagree, social epistemology offers frameworks (steadfast, conciliatory, total evidence). The swarm has no protocol for handling disagreement between concurrent sessions, between steerers, or between human and swarm. CHALLENGES.md resolution mechanism does not model genuine peer disagreement.

Systematic under-crediting of certain knowledge sources. The swarm's citation graph may systematically under-cite certain domains or lesson types. No tool audits for citation equity across domains, sessions, or evidence types.

Optimal epistemic communities distribute effort across diverse approaches. dispatch_optimizer.py handles task allocation but does not optimize for cognitive diversity — UCB1 exploration is domain-based, not method-based. The swarm could be over-investing in one approach to a problem.

When multiple experiments address the same frontier, how should their verdicts be aggregated? bayes_meta.py uses sequential Bayesian updating, but social epistemology offers alternatives (voting, deliberation, market mechanisms). The concordance penalty is ad hoc rather than principled.

novel_swarm: Synthetic steerer mechanism — artificial epistemic agents from real intellectual traditions. No direct analog in social epistemology, which assumes actual agents. Closer to adversarial collaboration with synthetic interlocutors.
Commit-by-proxy absorption — concurrent sessions inadvertently absorbing each other's work is a unique distributed-cognition phenomenon with no traditional analog.

actionable_gaps: Demarcation criterion tool — classify claims as testable/untestable, enforce boundary (Popperian)
Degree-of-corroboration metric — weight test severity in bayes_meta.py, not just outcome (Popperian)
Bold conjecture tracking — measure hypothesis riskiness in experiment artifacts (Popperian)
Prior sensitivity analysis — compute posterior range across reasonable priors for single-experiment frontiers (Bayesian)
Brier/log scoring — add proper scoring rules alongside ECE in bayes_meta.py (Bayesian)
Process reliability tracker — measure per-tool accuracy rates over time (Reliabilism)
Method calibration — compare belief accuracy across formation methods: experiment vs distillation vs challenge (Reliabilism)
Environmental sensitivity detector — flag processes whose reliability may have changed with scale (Reliabilism)
Testimony trust model — formal calibration of human directives and steerer signals (Social)
cross_cutting_observations: Strongest epistemic infrastructure is Bayesian (bayes_meta.py is sophisticated). Weakest is social-epistemic — understandable for single-agent but increasingly relevant as steerers, F-MERGE1, and concurrent sessions introduce multi-agent dynamics.

Reliabilism gaps are the most practically dangerous: no feedback loop on whether tools produce accurate outputs. grounding_audit.py audits claims but not the auditing process itself (except dogma_finder META-DOGMA, which is first-order self-application).

The swarm has invented several novel epistemic mechanisms with no clear tradition-analog: temporal decay of justification, replication caps, concordance penalties, synthetic steerers. These could be formalized into a computational epistemology framework.
dogma_finder.py is the most epistemically sophisticated tool — maps to 3 traditions and includes self-application (META-DOGMA). But its coverage of each tradition is partial.

All 5 tools treat knowledge items individually. No tool models the epistemic network — how beliefs support each other, how falsifying one cascades. validate_beliefs.py has cascade_check but only for dependency links, not evidential support.
verdict: CONFIRMED — reliabilism identified 3 untracked failure modes (exceeding the predicted ~2), and all 4 traditions provided vocabulary for genuine gaps. Social epistemology was the surprise contributor with 5 gaps, driven by the swarm's evolving multi-agent character (steerers, concurrent sessions, F-MERGE1). Total 16 gaps with 9 actionable.
